edges.description: Degeneration of premotor and supplementary motor speech networks produces progressive impairment of speech motor planning and programming.
PSP/CBD-spectrum 4-repeat tauopathy is the common upstream pathology associated with degeneration of the premotor and supplementary motor speech network.

nodes.description: Many PPAOS cases are associated with underlying 4-repeat tauopathy, particularly progressive supranuclear palsy or corticobasal degeneration, although non-tau pathology can occur.

evidence.reference_title: Amyloid and Tau PET Positivity in Progressive Agrammatic Aphasia and Apraxia of Speech.
evidence.snippet: These patients typically have an underlying 4-repeat tauopathy, although they sometimes show evidence of amyloid-β and tau deposition on PET, suggesting Alzheimer's disease (AD).

evidence.reference_title: Primary Progressive Apraxia of Speech: From Recognition to Diagnosis and Care.
evidence.snippet: Most published autopsied cases with an initial PPAOS diagnosis have had a 4-repeat (4R) tauopathy (a subcategory of proteinopathies), with CBD and PSP being the most common PPAOS-associated disease subtypes

nodes.description: Progressive degeneration of left-dominant motor speech planning networks impairs the sequencing and coordination of articulatory movements.

evidence.reference_title: Characterizing a neurodegenerative syndrome: primary progressive apraxia of speech.
evidence.snippet: Voxel-based morphometry of grey matter revealed focal atrophy of superior lateral premotor cortex and supplementary motor area.

nodes.description: Impaired planning and programming of speech movements produces distorted, effortful, or segmented speech.

evidence.reference_title: Characterizing a neurodegenerative syndrome: primary progressive apraxia of speech.
evidence.snippet: Apraxia of speech is a disorder of speech motor planning and/or programming that is distinguishable from aphasia and dysarthria.

evidence.reference_title: Primary Progressive Apraxia of Speech: From Recognition to Diagnosis and Care.
evidence.snippet: PPAOS can be more narrowly defined as AOS of insidious onset and gradual progression, in the absence of more than equivocal evidence of aphasia, nonaphasic cognitive impairments or sensorimotor deficits that meet criteria for another specific neurodegenerative disease or other explanatory condition at the time of diagnosis.

nodes.description: Behavioral speech-language intervention, compensatory strategies, and augmentative and alternative communication are used to maintain functional communication, although PPAOS-specific treatment evidence remains limited.

evidence.reference_title: Behavioral Treatment for Speech and Language in Primary Progressive Aphasia and Primary Progressive Apraxia of Speech: A Systematic Review.
evidence.snippet: The available evidence supports speech-language intervention for persons with PPA; however, treatment for PPAOS awaits systematic investigation.

evidence.reference_title: Primary Progressive Apraxia of Speech: From Recognition to Diagnosis and Care.
evidence.snippet: Beyond efforts to maintain or improve speech, staging of management to develop effective speaker and listener compensatory strategies, with early and ongoing involvement of partners, preferably in advance of reduced intelligibility, are essential and achievable in many cases.
